items.title: Confirm the symptom before deep profiling
items.avoid: Setting up memray on every script.
items.prefer: Watch RSS (`psutil`, dashboard) first; only break out tracemalloc/memray when you've confirmed unbounded growth or unreasonable peak.
items.why: Memory profiling is sharper than cProfile but more expensive — use it deliberately.
items.example: RSS dashboard shows growth → tracemalloc snapshots → fix → re-watch RSS.

items.title: Use snapshot diffs to find leaks
items.avoid: Reading totals and guessing what's leaking.
items.prefer: `tracemalloc.take_snapshot()` before and after a repeatable operation; `compare_to` to see growth.
items.why: Leaks are *delta* problems; totals don't reveal them.
items.example: Run the suspect op 10 times between snapshots; the diff names the leak site.

items.title: Prefer numpy / arrow for large homogeneous numeric data
items.why: Contiguous typed buffers are 20-50× smaller than equivalent Python objects.
items.example: Embedding store: numpy fp32 by default; fp16 / int8 quantisation if memory pressure remains.

items.title: Bound caches and shared structures
items.prefer: Explicit `maxsize`; TTL eviction for long-running services.
items.why: Unbounded caches are textbook leaks in long-running services.

items.title: Page indices from disk when they exceed RAM
items.avoid: Loading a 50 GB FAISS index entirely into memory.
items.prefer: mmap-backed indices (`faiss.IO_FLAG_MMAP`); on-disk vector stores; cloud-hosted vector DBs.
items.why: RAM is finite; mmap lets the kernel page in what's needed and out what isn't.
items.example: `faiss.read_index(path, faiss.IO_FLAG_MMAP)` for very large local indices.
